content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441 • •     322-6444

July 15, 1982


Deborah Hopkins
Assistant County Counsel
County of Santa Cruz
701 Ocean Street
Santa Cruz, CA  95060

Re: Your Request for Written Advice on Behalf of Ms. Judy
    Primavera and Dr. Joan Kayson, Members of the
    Santa Cruz School District Board of Education
    Our No. A-82-106

Dear Ms. Hopkins:

    Thank you for your letter requesting written advice.
In our subsequent telephone conversations you have stated
that you are seeking this advice as the authorized
representative for Dr. Joan Kayson and Ms. Judy Primavera,
as well as at the request of the Board of Education of the
Santa Cruz School District.

    You have presented the following two questions for our
analysis and advice.

                              Questions

    1.  Does a board member, whose spouse is a dentist
whose practice is limited to periodontics and is within the
territory of the school district, have a conflict of
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the
certificated and classified employees' employment
contracts, which contracts include provisions that the
district provide comprehensive dental insurance coverage
for the employees and their dependents?

    2.  Does a board member who, within the past 12 months,
borrowed $3,000 from a married couple of which one spouse
is a certificated employee and the other spouse is a
classified employee of the District, have a conflict of
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the
certificated and/or classified employees' employment
contracts?

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 2


Background Information Regarding District
Organization, the Bargaining Process and
The Employment Contracts

The Santa Cruz City High School District and the Santa
Cruz City Elementary School District are separate legal
entities that are governed by the same seven-member Board
of Education. The two districts operate jointly for all
purposes relevant to this inquiry and therefore will be
referred to collectively as "the District."

Persons employed by the District generally fall into
one of two basic categories: certificated employees and
classified employees. Each of these categories of
employees has an exclusive representative for collective
bargaining purposes. For the past several years the
District has entered into an employment agreement referred
to as a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with each
bargaining unit. The MOU for the certificated staff of the
high school district is identical to the MOU for the
certificated staff of the elementary school district.
Similarly, the MOU for the classified staff of the high
school district is identical to the MOU for the classified
staff of the elementary school district. The MOU's set
forth the various rights and responsibilities of the
respective parties and include provisions relating to, for
example, salaries and wages, fringe benefits, vacations and
leaves of absence, transfers, evaluations, grievance
procedures, and other terms and conditions of employment.

The local unit of the American Federation of Teachers
is the employee organization that is the exclusive
representative of the certificated employees of the
District. Approximately 325 persons are presently employed
in certificated positions pursuant to an agreement that is
effective through June 30, 1983. The local unit of the
California School Employees Association is the employee
organization that is the exclusive representative of the
classified employees of the district. The presently
effective agreement between the district and the
approximately 400 classified employees will expire on
August 31, 1982. Negotiations for a successor agreement
with the classified employees' bargaining unit and for
amendments pursuant to "reopener" clauses in the
certificated employees' contract are in progress.

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 3


     The employees' contracts are generally negotiated by
representatives of the bargaining unit and of the Board of
Education. Any proposed agreement or amendment thereto
that is tentatively accepted by the negotiating parties must
ultimately be approved and adopted by a majority of the
membership of the unit, as well as by a majority of the
members of the Board of Education. As noted above, the MOU
include many component parts. However, any proposed
employment agreement is presented to the Board as a package
and is either rejected or approved in its entirety in one
action by the Board.

          Specific Facts Regarding the Dental
               Insurance Component of the Fringe
               Benefits Provided by the Contract

     Since at least 1974, the District has provided some
form of dental insurance coverage for its employees. The
coverage under the programs that are currently being
provided commenced in January of 1979. While the particular
insurance carrier through which the coverage is provided
differs between the certificated and the classified
bargaining units, the programs are essentially the same.
Both programs are designed to encourage regular dental
checkups and preventive care, and both provide for increases
in the percentage of the charges that are covered, if the
patient/employee has annual examinations. Both programs
allow up to 100% coverage for a wide variety of dental
services, supplies and treatments, including periodontic
treatment. Under the program for certificated employees,
100% of the expenses charged by "member dentists" may be
covered; under the program for classified employees, 100%
of the expenses for "usual, customary and reasonable
charges," as determined by the carrier, may be covered.
Under both programs the dentist is selected entirely by
the patient/employee; claims may be processed by either the
patient/employee or by the dentist. Dependents of full-time
employees are covered under both programs.

     Each of these programs was adopted as a component of
the MOU between the Board of Education and the respective
collective bargaining units.

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 4


Specific Facts Regarding
The Subject Individuals

1. Judy Primavera: Ms. Judy Primavera was elected to
the Board of Education in April 1977. Ms. Primavera is
married to Dr. Carl E. Primavera, D.D.S. Dr. Primavera's
dentistry practice is located within the territory of the
school district and is limited to a specialization in
periodontics which pertains to diseases of the supporting
structures of the teeth. The business organization of the
practice is that of a corporation. Dr. Primavera is the
President and Treasurer of the corporation; Ms. Primavera
is the Vice President and Secretary. The practice is the
primary source of Dr. and Ms. Primavera's community
property income. Of the patients Dr. Primavera treats,
approximately 2% are Santa Cruz City School District
employees. Dr. Primavera is a "member dentist" as used in
the dental program for the school district's certificated
employees. There are approximately 140 practicing dentists
in Santa Cruz County. Dr. Primavera and one other limit
their practices to periodontic work; between 20 to 50 do
some periodontic work. There are, of course, other
dentists and other periodontists in neighboring Monterey
and Santa Clara Counties.

2. Joan Kayson: Dr. Joan Kayson was elected to the
Board of Education in April 1975. Dr. Kayson is the
president and owner of a private, independent profit making
post-secondary school that is doing business as "The
University of the States." The University is organized as
a sole proprietorship. It commenced operation in the fall
of 1980. The funds for the capital investment to finance
the origination and operation of the University derived
primarily from personal loans to Dr. Kayson from a number
of private individuals who reside in the Santa Cruz area.
Included in the amounts received was $3,000 borrowed from a
married couple of which one spouse is a certificated
employee and the other spouse is a classified employee of
the District. The loan was made within the past twelve
months. It was unsecured and was evidenced by a written
promissory note signed by Dr. Kayson and was to bear
interest at the rate of 22% per annum. The note was to be
repaid within one year and has, in fact, been repaid.

Deboran Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 5


                              ANALYSIS

     1.  Ms. Judy Primavera:

Source of Income

     Government Code Section 87100 provides that a
government official may not make, or participate in the
making of, a decision in which he or she has a financial
interest.  Section 87103 provides that an official has a
financial interest where it is reasonably foreseeable that
the financial effect of the decision will be material as
to:

          (c)  Any source of income, other than loans by a
     commercial lending institution in the regular course of
     business on terms available to the public without
     regard to official status, aggregating two hundred
     fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
     received by or promised to the public official within
     12 months prior to the time when the decision was
     made.2/

     In 1977, we considered the very similar situation of a
Dr. Black, a member of the Tahoe-Truckee School District
Board, who had a medical practice specializing in bone
fractures.  A copy of our advice letter, No. A-77-087, to
Douglas A. Lewis, Placer County Schools' Attorney is
enclosed.

     In the Lewis letter, we held that even though
Dr. Black's medical fees were often paid by the medical
insurance carrier, it was the patients who constituted the
source of income for Dr. Black.3/  In the instant case,

_____________________

     1/  All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.  The Political Reform Act
consists of Government Code Section 81000-91014.

     2/  Section 87103(c).

     3/  Here, Dr. Primavera is "member dentist" which
might constitute the "formal agreement or plan" of
referrals discussed in the Lewis letter regarding
Dr. Black.  Without substantially more information on this
subject we cannot determine if the exception would apply,
thereby making the insurance carrier a source of income as
well as the patient.

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 6


Ms. Primavera's circumstances are almost identical,
although only one-half1/ of Dr. Primavera's income from
any specific patient will be attributed to her. Thus,
before a district employee would be considered a source of
income to Ms. Primavera, that employee would have to have
paid Dr. Primavera $500 or more during the preceding
12-month period. Given her position as Vice President and
Secretary of the business, she is certainly in a position
to know if this is the case.

The focus of our analysis then must turn to the
question of whether the effect of the decision on the
employee contract on these patients will be material and
will be different from the effect on a significant segment
of the public.5/

We think that it is not reasonably foreseeable that a
decision on a wage and benefits package for an employee
contract will have such an effect on an individual employee
to be covered by that contract. These contracts are the
subject of intense negotiations and sometimes the cause of
strikes and other actions. It is not reasonably
foreseeable that contract terms would be altered solely
for the benefit of the individual. Thus, the real range of
decisional options is too narrow to permit the freedom to
have a significant effect on an individual employee.

In response to my telephone inquiry, you have looked
into the matter and have advised me that any certificated
or classified employees who are patients of Dr. Primavera
would simply be "unit members" in the two bargaining
units. Salary and benefit adjustments are negotiated on an
across-the-board basis, with no probability of these
individuals receiving treatment distinguishable from any
other member of the bargaining unit.6/

_________________
4/ This represents her community property share of
her spouse's income. Section 82030(a).

5/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18702 and 18703.

6/ The only exception is that sometimes certain
classifications have an additional step added because many
employees in that classification are at the top step. If a
step is added and if one of the individuals who is a source
of income will move up in that step, then our analysis
would change.

Deboran Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 7


     In the Lewis letter, we went on to advise that
Dr. Black could participate in decisions affecting wages
and benefits of employees who were sources of income to him
through his medical practice.  In reaching its conclusion
in the Lewis letter, the Commission considered the facts
that Dr. Black would be making district-wide decisions
which would not single out his patients for special
treatment.7/

Investment in Business Entity

     Ms. Primavera has an investment in Dr. Primavera's
dental practice in excess of $1,000;8/ she is an officer
of this business entity and it is a source of income to
her, as are Dr. Primavera's patients.  Therefore, several
of the tests in Section 87103 are satisfied and it is clear
that Ms. Primavera has a financial interest on more than
one basis.

     As to Ms. Primavera's financial interest in her
husband's business, that issue, too, was resolved in the
Lewis letter.9/  Because of Dr. Primavera's
specialization and because some 20 to 50 other dentists
also do periodontic work, we conclude that the Lewis

_____________________

     7/ Specifically, in the Lewis letter we said, as follows:

          Since some employees of the District are sources of
     income to Dr. Black, the question might arise as to whether
     Dr. Black may participate in decisions concerning matters
     such as employee wages, terms and conditions of employment,
     disciplinary actions, etc.  We do not think that the Act
     should be read as prohibiting Dr. Black from participating
     in this wide range of Board actions.

          Lewis letter, supra, at page 5.

     8/ We make this assumption without the necessity of it
having been stated in your letter.

     9/ Lewis letter, supra, at pp. 3-5.

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 8


analysis10/ is applicable here and that there will be no
conflict arising from Ms. Primavera's participation in
consideration of health and welfare plans for district
employees, unless such a decision focussed specifically
upon periodontic coverage.

2. Dr. Joan Kayson: Clearly, Dr. Kayson is a public
official and the couple who loaned her the $3,000 is a
source of income to her of more than $250 during the
12-month period preceding the August 31, 1982, Contract
decision; that period being measured from the date the loan
was repaid.11/

Consequently, Dr. Kayson's situation is the same as
that analyzed above for Ms. Primavera and there would be no
conflict as to across-the-board wage and benefit
decisions.12/

_________________

10/ Id.

11/ The 12-month period runs from whenever the
outstanding loan balance fell below $250 per lender. As to
the decision next year, on the certificated employee, 12
months may very well have expired prior to the point of
involvement of Dr. Kayson in the decisional process, in
which case the couple would not be a source of income to
Dr. Kayson and there would be no basis for a conflict.

12/ However, this is based upon the specific facts
in this case, where we understand the loan from two
District employees was an isolated incident and not a part
of some "pattern or practice." If several members of a
bargaining unit were sources of income to Dr. Kayson, our
conclusion might change. We have also been advised by you
that these two employees are also simply "unit members" of
their respective bargaining units.

Deborah Hopkins
July 15, 1982
Page 9


CONCLUSION

Neither Ms. Primavera nor Dr. Kayson will have a
conflict of interest in participating in broad,
district-wide employee benefit decisions, so long as there
is no focus on the specific individuals who are sources of
income and so long as those individuals are de minimus in
number in comparison to the entire bargaining unit.
Ms. Primavera will not have a conflict of interest in
participating in health and welfare decisions so long as
periodontic care is not the focus of deliberations.

If you have any questions please feel free to call me
at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:km

OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY COUNSEL

COUNTY OF SANTA CRUZ

GOVERNMENTAL CENTER

701 OCEAN STREET

SANTA CRUZ, CALIFORNIA 95060

(408) 425-2041

CLAIR A. CARLSON
COUNTY COUNSEL

May 26, 1982

JAMES M. RITCHEY
JONATHAN WITTWER
REBECCA J. CLEWETT
DEBORAH HOPKINS
ASSISTANTS

DWIGHT L. HERR
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

Barbara Milman
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
of the State of California
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re:  Request for Written Advice Concerning Adoption of
Employment Contracts with the Collective Bargaining
Units of the Certificated and Classified Employees
of the Santa Cruz City School Districts.

Dear Ms. Milman:

     The Board of Education of the Santa Cruz School District has
requested that this office seek your opinion as to whether a con-
flict of interest exists with respect to the adoption of the employ-
ment contracts with the collective bargaining units for the certifi-
cated and classified employees of the Santa Cruz City School Districts.
Accordingly, request is hereby made pursuant to the Political Reform
Act of 1974, and specifically Government Code Section 83114(b), for
your written advice as to whether either or both of the particular
factual situations set forth below regarding two members of the board
of Education gives rise to an impermissible conflict of interest.

QUESTION

     The question presented is whether or not a conflict of interest
exists within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100, et seq.,
so as to prohibit either or both of the individuals mentioned herein
from making a decision, participating in making a decision, or using
their positions to influence the making of a decision regarding
the approval of the subject employment contracts. The specific
questions presented for your review and advice are:

     1. Does a Board Member who, within the past 12 months,
borrowed $3000 from a married couple of which one spouse is a
certificated employee and the other spouse is a classified employee
of the District, have a conflict of interest with regard to the
Board's adoption of the certificated and/or classified employees'
employment contracts?

Ms. Barbara Milman
May 26, 1982
Page two

Re: Request for Written Advice Concerning Adoption of
Employment Contracts with the Collective Bargaining
Units of the Certificated and Classified Employees
of the Santa Cruz City School Districts.

2. Does a Board Member whose spouse is a dentist whose prac-
tice is limited to periodontics and is within the territory of the school
district have a conflict of interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the
certificated and classified employees' employment contracts, which contracts include provisions that the district pro-vide comprehensive dental insurance coverage for the employees and
their dependents?

BACKGROUND INFORMATION REGARDING DISTRICT
ORGANIZATION, THE BARGAINING PROCESS AND
THE EMPLOYMENT CONTRACTS

The Santa Cruz City High School District and the Santa Cruz
City Elementary School District are separate legal entities that are governed by the same seven-member Board of Education. The two districts operate jointly for all purposes relevant to this inquiry and therefore will be referred to collectively as "the District".

Persons employed by the District generally fall into one of two basic categories: certificated employees and classified employees. Each of these categories of employees has an exclusive representative for collective bargaining purposes. For the past several years the District has entered into an employment agreement referred to as a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with each bargaining unit. The MOU for the certificated staff of the high school district is identical to the MOU for the certificated staff of the elementary school district. Similarly, the MOU for the classified staff of the high school district is identical to the MOU for the classified staff of the elementary school district. The MOU's set forth the various rights and responsibilities of the respective parties and include provisions relating to, for example, salaries and wages, fringe benefits, vacations and leave of absence, transfers, evaluations, grievance procedures, and other terms and conditions of employment.

The local unit of the American Federation of Teachers is the employee organization that is the exclusive representative of the certificated employees of the District. Approximately 325 persons are presently employed in certificated positions pursuant to an agreement that is effective through June 30, 1983. The local unit of the California School Employees Association is the employee organization that is the exclusive representative of the classified employees of the district. The presently effective agreement between the district and the approximately 400 classified employees

Ms. Barbara Milman
May 26, 1982
Page three

Re: Request for Written Advice Concerning Adoption of
Employment Contracts with the Collective Bargaining
Units of the Certificated and Classified Employees
of the Santa Cruz City School Districts.

will expire on August 31, 1982. Negotiations for a successor agree-
ment with the classified employees' bargaining unit and for amend-
ments pursuant to "reopener" clauses in the certificated employees'
contract are in progress.

The employees' contracts are generally negotiated by represen-
tatives of the bargaining unit and of the Board of Education.
Any proposed agreement or amendment thereto that is tentatively
accepted by the negotiators must ultimately be approved and adopted
by a majority of the membership of the unit as well as by a majority
of the members of the Board of Education. As noted above, the
Memoranda of Understanding include many component parts. However,
any proposed employment agreement is presented to the Board as a
package and is either rejected or approved in its entirely in one
action by the Board.

SPECIFIC FACTS REGARDING THE DENTAL
INSURANCE COMPONENT OF THE FRINGE
BENEFITS PROVIDED BY THE CONTRACT

Since at least 1974, the District has provided some form of
dental insurance coverage for its employees. The coverage under the
programs that are currently being provided commenced in January
of 1979. While the particular insurance carrier through which the
coverage is provided differs between the certificated and the classi-
fied bargaining units, the programs are essentially the same. Both
programs are designed to encourage regular dental checkups and
preventive care and both provide for increases in the percentage
of the charges that are covered if the patient/employee has annual
examinations. Both programs allow up to 100 percent coverage for a
wide variety of dental services, supplies and treatments, including
periodontic treatment. Under the program for certificated employees,
100 percent of the expenses charged by "member dentists" may be
covered; under the program for classified employees, 100 percent of
the expenses for "usual, customary and reasonable charges," as de-
termined by the carrier, may be covered. Under both programs the
dentist is selected entirely by the patient/employee; claims may be
processed by either the patient/employee or by the dentist. De-
pendents of full-time employees are covered under both programs.

Each of these programs was adopted as a component of the Memorandum of Understanding between the Board of Education and the res-
pective collective bargaining units.

Ms. Barbara Milman
May 26, 1982
Page four

Re:  Request for Written Advice Concerning Adoption of
     Employment Contracts with the Collective Bargaining
     Units of the Certificated and Classified Employees
     of the Santa Cruz City School Districts

                    SPECIFIC FACTS REGARDING
                    THE SUBJECT INDIVIDUALS

     1.  JOAN KAYSON:  Dr. Joan Kayson was elected to the Board of
Education in April, 1975.  Dr. Kayson is the president and owner
of a private, independent profit making post-secondary school that
is doing business as "The University of the States".  The University
is organized as a sole proprietorship.  It commenced operation in
the fall of 1980.  The funds for the capital investment to finance
the origination and operation of the University derived primarily
from personal loans to Dr. Kayson from a number of private individuals
who reside in the Santa Cruz area.  Included in the amounts received
was $3,000 borrowed from a married couple of which one spouse is a
certificated employee and the other spouse is a classified employee
of the District.  The loan was made within the past twelve months.
It was unsecured and was evidenced by a written promissory note signed
by Dr. Kayson and was to bear interest at the rate of 22 percent per
annum.  The note was to be repaid within one year and has, in fact,
been repaid.

     2.  JUDY PRIMAVERA:  Ms. Judy Primavera was elected to the Board
of Education in April, 1977.  Ms. Primavera is married to Dr. Carl
Primavera, D.D.S.  Dr. Primavera's dentistry practice is located
within the territory of the school district and is limited to a
specialization in periodontics which pertains to diseases of the
supporting structures of the teeth.  The business organization of the
practice is that of a corporation.  Dr. Primavera is the President
and Treasurer of the corporation; Ms. Primavera is the Vice President
and Secretary.  The practice is the primary source of Dr. and Ms.
Primavera's community property income.  Of the patients Dr. Prima-
vera treats, approximately 2 percent are Santa Cruz City School
District employees.  Dr. Primavera is a "member dentist" as used
in the dental program for the school district's certificated employ-
ees.  There are approximately 140 practicing dentists in Santa Cruz
County.  Dr. Primavera and one other limit their practices to peri-
iodontic work; between 20 to 50 do some periodontic work.  There are,
of course, other dentists and other periodontists in neighboring
Monterey and Santa Clara Counties.

     It is hoped that the information provided herein is sufficient
for your purposes.  Should you have any questions or need additional
information, please contact me at this office.

Ms. Barbara Milman
May 26, 1982
Page five

Re:  Request for Written Advice Concerning Adoption of
     Employment Contracts with the Collective Bargaining
     Units of the Certificated and Classified Employees
     of the Santa Cruz City School Districts

     Your prompt reply to our inquiry would be very much appreciated.
Thank you for your courtesy and cooperation in this regard.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        CLAIR A. CARLSON, COUNTY COUNSEL

                                        BY: [signature]
                                            DEBORAH HOPKINS
                                            Assistant County Counsel

cc:  Dr. Joan Kayson
     Ms. Judy Primavera
     Dr. Dale Kinsley, Supt.

sections.question:
1. 
Does a board member, whose spouse is a dentist 
whose practice is limited to periodontics and is within the 
territory of the school district, have a conflict of 
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the 
certificated and classified employees' employment 
contracts, which contracts include provisions that the 
district provide comprehensive dental insurance coverage 
for the employees and their dependents? 
2. 
Does a board member who, within the past 12 months, 
borrowed $3,000 from a married couple of which one spous-e 
is a certificated employee and the other spouse is a 
classified employee of the District, have a conflict of 
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the 
certificated and/or classified employees' employment 
contracts? 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982
sections.conclusion:
Neither Ms. Primavera nor Dr. Kayson will have a 
conflict of interest in participating in broad, 
district-wide employee benefit decisions, so long as there 
is no focus on the specific individuals who are sources of 
income and so long as those individuals are de minimus in 
number in comparison to the entire bargaining-unit. 
Ms. Primavera will not have a conflict of interest in 
participating in health and welfare decisions so long as 
periodontic care is not the focus of deliberations.

The Employment Contracts

Cruz City Elementary School District are separate legal 
entities that are governed by the same seven-member Board 
of Education. 
The two districts operate jointly for all 
purposes relevant to this inquiry and therefore will be 
referred to collectively as "the District." 
Persons employed by the District generally fall into 
one of two basic categories: 
certificated employees and 
classified employees. 
Each of these categories of 
employees has an exclusive representative for collective 
bargaining purposes. 
For the past several years the 
District has entered into an employment agreement referred 
to as a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with each 
bargaining unit. 
The MOU for the certificated staff of the 
high school district is identical to the MOU for the 
certificated staff of the elementary school district. 
Similarly, the MOU for the classified staff of the high 
school district is identical to the MOU for the classified 
staff of the elementary school district. 
The MOU's set 
forth the various rights and responsibilities of the 
respective parties and include provisions relating to, for 
example, salaries and wages, fringe benefits, vacations and 
leaves of absence, transfers, evaluations, grievance 
procedures, and other terms and conditions of employment. 
The local unit of the American Federation of Teachers 
is the employee organization that is the exclusive 
representative of the certificated employees of the 
District. 
Approximately 325 persons are presently employed 
in certificated positions pursuant to an agreement that is 
effective through June 30, 1983. 
The local unit of the 
California School Employees Association is the employee 
organization that is the exclusive representative of the 
classified employees of the district. 
The presently 
effective agreement between the district and the 
approximately 400 classified employees will expire on 
August 31, 1982. 
Negotiations for a successor agreement 
with the classified employees' bargaining unit and for 
amendments pursuant to "reopener" clauses in the 
certificated employees' contract are in progress. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
The employees' contracts are generally negotiated by 
representatives of the bargaining unit and of the Board of 
Education. 
Any proposed agreement or amendment thereto 
that is tentatively accepted by the negotiators must 
ultimately be approved and adopted by a majority of the 
membership of the unit, as well as by a majority of the 
members of the Board of Education. 
As noted above, the MOU 
include many component parts. 
However, any proposed 
employment agreement is presented to the Board as a package 
and is either rejected or approved in its entirety in one 
action by the Board. 
Specific Facts Regarding the' Dental 
Insurance Component of the Fringe 
Benefits Provided by the Contract 
Since at least 1974, the District has provided some 
form of dental insurance coverage for its employees. 
The 
coverage under the programs that are currently being 
provided commenced in January of 1979. 
While the 
particular insurance carrier through which the coverage is 
provided differs between the certificated and the

the same. 
Both programs are designed to encourage regular

increases in the percentage of the charges that are 
covered, if the patient/employee has annual examinations. 
Both programs allow up to 100% coverage for a wide variety 
of dental services, supplies and treatments, including

Under the program for certificated 
employees, 100% of the expenses charged by "member 
dentists" may be covered; under the program for classified 
employees, 100% of the expenses for "usual, customary and 
reasonable charges," as determined by the carrier, may be 
covered. 
Under both programs the dentist is selected 
entirely by the patient/employee; claims may be processed 
by either the patient/employee or by the dentist. 
Dependents of full-time employees are covered under both 
programs. 
Each of these programs was adopted as a component of 
the MOU between the Board of Education and the respective 
collective bargaining units. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
Specific Facts Regarding 
The Subject Individuals 
1. 
Judy Primavera: 
Ms. Judy Primavera was elected to 
the Board of Education in April 1977. 
Ms. Primavera is 
married to Dr. Carl E. Primavera, D.D.S. 
Dr. Primavera's 
dentistry practice is located within the territory of the 
school district and is limited to a specialization in 
periodontics which pertains to diseases of the supporting 
structures of the teeth. 
The business organization of the 
practice is that of a corporation. 
Dr. Primavera is the 
President and Treasurer of the corporation; Ms. Primavera 
is the Vice President and Secretary. 
The practice is the 
primary source of Dr. and Ms. Primavera's community 
property income. 
Of the patients Dr. Primavera treats, 
approximately 2% are Santa Cruz City School District 
employees. 
Dr. Primavera is a "member dentist" as used in 
the dental program for the school district's certificated 
employees. 
There are approximately 140 practicing dentists 
in Santa Cruz County. 
Dr. Primavera and one other limit 
their practices to periodontic work; between 20 to 50 do 
some periodontic work. 
There are, of course, other 
dentists and other periodontists in neighboring Monterey 
and Santa Clara Counties. 
2. 
Joan Kayson: 
Dr. Joan Kayson was elected to the 
Board of Education in April 1975. 
Dr. Kayson is the 
president and owner of a private, independent profit making 
post-secondary school that is doing business as "The 
University of the States." 
The University is organized as 
a sole proprietorship. 
It commenced operation in the fall 
of 1980. 
The funds for the capital investment to finance 
the origination and operation of the University derived 
primarily from personal loans to Dr. Kayson from a number 
of private individuals who reside in the Santa Cruz area. 
Included in the amounts received was $3,000 borrowed from a 
married couple of which one spouse is a certificated 
employee and the other spouse is a classified employee of 
the District. 
The loan was made within the past twelve 
months. 
It was unsecured and was evidenced by a written 
promissory note signed by Dr. Kayson and was to bear 
interest at the rate of 22% per annum. 
The note was to be 
repaid within one year and has, in fact, been repaid. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
1. 
Ms. Judy Primavera: 
Source of Income
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 8710011 provides that a 
government official may not make, or participate in the 
making of, a aecision in which he or she has a financial 
interest. 
Section 87103 provides that an official has a 
financial interest where it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the financial effect of the decision will be material as 
to: 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision was 
made.~1 
In 1977, we considered the very similar situation of a 
Dr. Black, a member of the Tahoe-Truckee School District 
Boara, who had a medical practice specializing in bone 
fractures. 
A copy of our advice letter, No. A-77-087, to 
Douglas A. Lewis, Placer County Schools' Attorney is 
enclosed. 
In the Lewis letter, we hela that even though 
Dr. Black's medical fees were often paid by the medical 
insurance carrier, it was the patients who constituted the 
source of income for Dr. Black.11 
In the instant case, 
11 
All statutory references are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise specified. 
The Political Reform Act 
consists of Government Code Section 81000-91014. 
21 
Section 87103{c). 
11 Here, Dr. Primavera is "member dentist" which 
might constitute the "formal agreement or plan" of 
referrals discussed in the Lewis letter regarding 
Ur. Black. 
Without substantially more information on this 
subject we cannot determine if the exception would apply, 
thereby making the insurance carrier a source of income as 
well as the patient. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
Ms. Primavera's circumstances are almost identical, 
although only one-halfi/ of Dr. Primavera's income from 
any specific patient will be attributed to her. 
Thus, 
before a district employee would be considered a source of 
income to Ms. Primavera, that employee would have to have 
paid Dr. Primavera $500 or more during the preceding 
12-month period. 
Given her position as Vice President and 
Secretary of the business, she is certainly in a position 
to know if this is the case. 
The focus of our analysis then must turn to the 
question of whether the effect of the decision on the 
employee contract on these patients will be material and 
will be different from the effect on a significant segment 
of the public.~/ 
We think that it is not reasonably foreseeable that a 
decision on a wage and benefits package for an employee 
contract will have such an effect on an individual employee 
to be covered by that contract. 
These contracts are the 
subject of intense negotiations and sometimes the cause of 
strikes and other actions. 
It is not reasonably 
foreseeable that the contract terms would be altered solely 
for the benefit of the individual. 
Thus, the real range of 
decisional options is too narrow to permit the freedom to 
have a significant effect on an individual employee. 
In response to my telephone inquiry, you have looked 
into the matter and have advised me that any certificated 
or classlfied employees who are patients of Dr. Primavera 
would simply be "unit members" in the two bargaining 
units. 
Salary and benefit adjustments are negotiated on an 
across-the-board basis, with no probability of these 
individuals receiving treatment distinguishable from any 
other member of the bargaining unit.~/ 
!/ This represents her community property share of 
her spouse's income. 
Section 82030(a) . 
~/ 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18702 and 18703. 
~/ The only exception is that sometimes certain 
classifications have an additional step added because many 
employees in that classification are at the top step. 
If a 
step is added and if one of the individuals who is a source 
of income will move up in that step, then our analysis 
would change. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
In the Lewis letter, we went on to advise that 
Dr. Black could participate in decisions affecting wages 
and benefits of employees who were sources of income to him 
through his medical practice. 
In reaching its conclusion 
in the Lewis letter, the Commission considered the facts 
that Dr. Black would be making district-wide decisions 
which would not single out his patients for special 
treatment.21 
Investment in Business Entity 
Ms. Primavera has an investment in Dr. Primavera's 
dental practice in excess of $l,OOOi~1 she is an officer 
of this business entity and it is a source of income to 
her, as are Dr. Primavera's patients. 
Therefore, several 
of the tests in Section 87103 are satisfied and it is clear 
that Ms. Primavera has a financial interest on more than 
one basis. 
As to Ms. Primavera's financial interest in her 
husband's business, that issue, too, was resolved in the 
Lewis letter.21 Because of Dr. Primavera's 
specialization and because some 20 to 50 other dentists 
also do periodontic work, we conclude that the Lewis 
21 
Specifically, in the Lewis letter we said, as follows: 
Since some employees of the District are sources of 
income to Dr. Black, the question might arise as to whether 
Dr. Black may participate in decisions concerning matters 
such as employee wages, terms and conditions of employment, 
disciplinary actions, etc. 
We do not think that the Act 
should be read as prohibiting Dr. Black from participating 
in this wide range of Board actions. 
Lewis letter, supra, at page 5. 
~I We make this assumption without the necessity of it 
having been stated in your letter. 
21 
Lewis letter, supra, at pp. 3-5. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982  
analysislQl is applicable here and that there will be no 
conflict arising from Ms. Primavera's participation in 
consideration of health and welfare plans for district 
employees, unless such a decision focussed specifically 
upon periodontic coverage. 
2. 
Dr. Joan Kayson: 
Clearly, Dr. Kayson is a public 
official and the couple who loaned her the $3,000 is a 
source of income to her of more than $250 during the 
12-month period preceding the August 31, 1982, contract 
decision; that period being measured from the date the loan 
was repaid. lll 
Consequently, Dr. Kayson's situation is the same as 
that analyzed above for Ms. Primavera and there would be no 
conflict as to across-the-board wage and benefit 
decisions. 121 
101 
Id. 
III The 12-month period runs from whenever the 
outstanding loan balance fell below $250 per lender. 
As to 
the decision next year, on the certificated employees, 12 
months may very well have expired prior to the point of 
involvement of Dr. Kayson in the decisional process, in 
which case the couple would not be a source of income to 
Dr. Kayson and there would be no basis for a conflict. 
121 
However, this is based upon the specific facts 
in this case, where we understand the loan from two 
District employees was an isolated incident and not a part 
of some "pattern or practice." 
If several members of a 
bargaining unit were sources of income to Dr. Kayson, our 
conclusion might change. 
We have also been advised by you 
that these two employees are also simply "unit members" of 
their respective bargaining units. 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does a board member, whose spouse is a dentist 
whose practice is limited to periodontics and is within the 
territory of the school district, have a conflict of 
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the 
certificated and classified employees' employment 
contracts, which contracts include provisions that the 
district provide comprehensive dental insurance coverage 
for the employees and their dependents? 
2. 
Does a board member who, within the past 12 months, 
borrowed $3,000 from a married couple of which one spous-e 
is a certificated employee and the other spouse is a 
classified employee of the District, have a conflict of 
interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the 
certificated and/or classified employees' employment 
contracts? 


Deborah Hopkins 
July 15, 1982

CONCLUSION: Neither Ms. Primavera nor Dr. Kayson will have a 
conflict of interest in participating in broad, 
district-wide employee benefit decisions, so long as there 
is no focus on the specific individuals who are sources of 
income and so long as those individuals are de minimus in 
number in comparison to the entire bargaining-unit. 
Ms. Primavera will not have a conflict of interest in 
participating in health and welfare decisions so long as 
periodontic care is not the focus of deliberations.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 • • 322-6444 July 15, 1982 Deborah Hopkins Assistant County Counsel County of Santa Cruz 701 Ocean Street Santa Cruz, CA 95060 Re: Your Request for Written Advice on Behalf of Ms. Judy Primavera and Dr. Joan Kayson, Members of the Santa Cruz School District Board of Education Our No. A-82-106 Dear Ms. Hopkins: Thank you for your letter requesting written advice. In our subsequent telephone conversations you have stated that you are seeking this advice as the authorized representative for Dr. Joan Kayson and Ms. Judy Primavera, as well as at the request of the Board of Education of the Santa Cruz School District. You have presented the following two questions for our analysis and advice. Questions 1. Does a board member, whose spouse is a dentist whose practice is limited to periodontics and is within the territory of the school district, have a conflict of interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the certificated and classified employees' employment contracts, which contracts include provisions that the district provide comprehensive dental insurance coverage for the employees and their dependents? 2. Does a board member who, within the past 12 months, borrowed $3,000 from a married couple of which one spouse is a certificated employee and the other spouse is a classified employee of the District, have a conflict of interest with regard to the Board's adoption of the certificated and/or classified employees' employment contracts? Deborah Hopkins July 15, 1982 Page 2 Background Information Regarding District Organization, the Bargaining Process and The Employment Contracts The Santa Cruz City High School District and the Santa Cruz City Elementary School District are separate legal entities that are governed by the same seven-member Board of Education. The two districts operate jointly for all purposes relevant to this inquiry and therefore will be referred to collectively as "the District." Persons employed by the District generally fall into one of two basic categories: certificated employees and classified employees. Each of these categories of employees has an exclusive representative for collective bargaining purposes. For the past several years the District has entered into an employment agreement referred to as a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with each bargaining unit. The MOU for the certificated staff of the high school district is identical to the MOU for the certificated staff of the elementary school district. Similarly, the MOU for the classified staff of the high school district is identical to the MOU for the classified staff of the elementary school district. The MOU's set forth the various rights and responsibilities of the respective parties and include provisions relating to, for example, salaries and wages, fringe benefits, vacations and leaves of absence, transfers, evaluations, grievance procedures, and other terms and conditions of employment. The local
